content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions month

## 1. Primer (2004) is a genius, low-budget sci-fi that demands your full attention.

**Score:** 439
**Body:** This is one of the most meticulously crafted time-travel movies ever made. It was shot on a tiny budget but feels incredibly smart and complex.  
You'll need to watch it more than once to piece everything together, and that's the best part of the experience.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I watched this once, thought I was following it, then in a moment had no idea what was happening
  - Being an edgy teen i was like “i fully understand this movie”. Then i opened the graphic someone made of all the timelines and i was oblivious to 95% of them lol

One of my first big humblings in life 
  - it's pretty much impossible to follow. i will preface by saying i think primer is the best time travel movie ever made, but it's biggest strength is also it's biggest flaw; it's just too complex. as it should be, multiverse time travel theory is crazy. it also means the movie has great rewatch value, but you pretty much need to either watch an explanation after or take notes while watching and really scrutinize every word. the script is very tight and fast so every line of dialogue has purpose, and they don't ever give you a moment to breathe and digest, which just makes it even harder to follow. when you do get it though, it is so rewarding.
  - I've seen it more than once, but same thing
    - I've seen it like, 6 times, watched youtube videos explaining it, studied infographics. . . and yeah, same thing
      - My favorite review of this film said "I don't want to be spoon-fed, but at least unlock the kitchen door for me!"
2. One of the greatest science fiction kicked ever made. Doubly impressive that is was made for only $7000.

Primer was the only movie which I've ever rewatched immediately after watching it for the first time. Then I watched it three more times. After all that I think I understand about 80% of it.
3. The director was on his way to being a new Kubrick IMHO, but ended up stalking his girlfriend/starlet and has since disappeared. If I'm wrong does anyone have updates on what he's involved in?
  - Yeah it’s such a disappointment that he disappeared under dodgy circumstances. I love Upstream Color maybe just as much as Primer, and then I read his screenplay for ‘A Topiary’ I think it was called. It was incredible. That would have been of the charts if he could have gone on to make it. Although I think he described it as impossible to make anyway.
4. The dvd with the director commentary is key to understanding that movie. I love it 

## 2. Movies where the reboot is better than the original?

**Score:** 324
**Body:** Currently watching Total Recall (2012) and boy is it awful 😂. I’m a big fan of the original Total Recall. 

I was quick to write of reboots as all together bad, then realized another of my favorites (DUNE) is waaaay better than the original. 

Seems like a pretty rare fluke that budgets, casts, etc align in such a way that Hollywood produces a better product decades removed from the source material.  

What other remakes are better than the original? 


### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The two amazing body-horror 80s movie remakes did this. The Fly and The Thing. Both remakes of B movies from the 50s. (The originals are decent, just nothing like the amazing remakes).


The Thomas Crown Affair remake (Piers Brosnan) is probably better than the original (Steve McQueen).


Same with Ocean's 11.
  - Toss The Blob in there, too.
    - Toss in Little Shop of Horrors while we are at it.
  - Invasion of the Body Snatchers with Donald Sutherland is also a *chef’s kiss* remake
2. Dredd (2012)… and it’s not close!
3. Heat (1995) is much better than L.A. Takedown (1989), a film a lot of people probably don't even know exists.
4. The Thing (1982), The Fly (1986), Airplane! (1980), Little Shop of Horrors (1986), The Blob (1988).
5. I always thought the 70’s Invasion of the Body Snatchers was better than the 50’s film.
6. Reboot implies a franchise. You probably mean remakes

True Grit (2010) is among my favorite films. And I say this as a person that used to hate Westerns
7. Ocean's 11

## 3. I need to get off my phone and watch movies again. Recommend me something, if it gets 10 upvotes, I’ll watch it and reply with what I thought of it.

**Score:** 267
**Body:** I feel like I haven’t watched anything good lately, most of the time I’m half-watching some trash movie or TV shows while scrolling on my phone. I need some strong recommendations. I’m open to pretty much anything.

Drop your best pick and I’ll give it a try, then come back with my feedback.

**Edit:**

Holy s\*\*t, I wasn’t expecting so many of you guys to give me movie suggestions. I am a man of my word though, and I’ll be watching every single movie that gets 10 upvotes or more. I’ll be watching at leas one movie a day until the list is completed, and I’ll reply to your comment before I watch the movie and again afterward with my thoughts on it.

The order in which I’ll be watching the movies will be decided by the number of upvotes, so I’ll go from the most upvoted comment to the least. So stay tuned and be patient, the list is now in the mind-blowing range of 100+ movies, but your turn will come, I promise.

**Movies watched & reviews:**  
\- Children of men  
\- Office Space  
\- Little Miss Sunshine  
\- Best in Show  
\- Blade Runner  
\- My Cousin Vinny  
\- The Princess Bride  
\- The Silence of the Lambs

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tombstone 
2. Best In Show
3. Jackie Brown
4. Office Space 
5. Harold & Maude
6. My Cousin Vinny
7. Blade Runner.  Sci-fi isn’t usually my fav but this was good.

Goodfellas is always entertaining
8. Children of men
9. The Thing
10. The Silence of the Lambs

## 4. Movies where the hero is actually the villain if you think about it

**Score:** 259
**Body:** I’m looking for movies where the protagonist is the real villain, either because of their actions, mindset, or a twist in perspective.

Not talking about anti-heroes like Joker or Deadpool, but some movies where:

- the story frames them as the hero,
- but the more you think about it,
- you realize they’re actually the problem.

Examples I’ve seen: Taxi Driver, Gone Girl, American Psycho, Nightcrawler.

Anything similar? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mrs doubt fire

Justice for Sally field!
  - Justice for Pierce Brosnan too!
2. In what world is Patrick Bateman a hero? It’s pretty blatant that he was never a good guy?
3. "The Talented Mr. Ripley"

I found myself hoping he'd get away, but then realized, hey this guy's a psycho!
4. Mrs. Doubtfire makes the other adults to be the problem/grumps, when it's really Robin Williams' man-child character.
5. *Memento*
6. Ferris Bueller's Day Off
7. Hang on, are you saying American Psycho frames it's protagonist as a hero?
8. There will be blood
  - Daniel is the protagonist but also clearly the villain

## 5. Movies that made you laugh so hard you cried

**Score:** 223
**Body:** I feel like I've exhausted the extent of my own repertoire/reserve of funny movies. I'd like to give something new a chance, so here I am, hoping you can help.

To give you an example of what I find funny, the new Naked Gun had me in tears at times (and I love the originals). The Office (US) is my favorite TV show of all time, so movies in that vein would work, too.

Edited to add: Y'all are amazing, thank you so much. I'm really enjoying the suggestions, especially your anecdotes around them. I'm getting some good ones! Keep it up!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Airplane, surely
  - Don't call me Shirley!
2. The birdcage
  - It’s da choes. They make me fall down.
3. 40 yr old virgin
4. Tropic Thunder
5. The Hangover....but ONLY if you haven't seen it. I was still laughing 2 days later.
6. Young Frankenstein.  Mr Mom.   Let it Ride.   
7. [deleted]
8. What we do in the shadows. The opening scene with the alarm clock. I had to pause it to gather myself and wipe away the tears.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic month

## 1. Very strange indeed.

**Score:** 11326

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Avatar is an experience, not a symbol. Marvel and Star Wars spread through symbols that people use every day. Avatar gives you a great feeling in the theatre, but there is nothing you carry with you afterward. That is why it does not stick.

In simple terms: it is like comparing Cirque du Soleil to a football game. A team carries symbols, identity, and shorthand that continue long after the game ends. Cirque du Soleil is extremely popular, but its impact is contained within the experience itself rather than spreading through shared cultural symbols.
  - That is a great analogy.
    - Also on a related note. They are blue aliens. Not overly relatable as Americas Ass or Luke.
      - The lack of actual human actors also contributed to this. People love RDJ, the character and the man are one. 

These are a bunch of blue things.
        - This is true. And while everybody loves Groot, if the 1st MCU movie was about a bunch of sentient trees, it wouldn’t resonate as much.
          - Sentient trees make great side characters just look at tree beard
            - “Tree? I am no tree! I am an ent!”
  - This is the kindest way anyone has described Avatar. Gotta keep this analogy in mind next time I'm about to hate on something
2. I think the 15 year time gap played a hand. To me, it felt very culturally buzzy the first few years after it released.
3. It isn't a memeable movie. People don't watch it for its unique characters or original story, they watch it for its visuals and worldbuilding. it is like an alien nature documentary with a plot.

## 2. What's the most cruel thing you've seen in a movie?

**Score:** 9009
**Body:** One scene in particular I could think of is this one from 1408 with John Cusack where his character "reunites" with his daughter for a short amount of time. Won't say anything else to those who haven't seen it!

EDIT: Goddamn, I never thought my post would get so big lol.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "She begged for her life and for the life of the baby inside her. Ohhhh, he didn't know."

- John Doe in Se7en
  - CALIFORNIA STAY AWAY FROM HERE. JOHN DOE HAS THE UPPER HAND
2. The Green Mile, Del’s execution.
3. Shoe, roger rabbit
  - The Dip!
  - STOP. Lol I block this one out for a few months at a time but it always randomly comes creeping back & I feel a sense of panic because I can't save him. I'm 42.
4. Baseball Boy from Dr. Sleep.

Horrible
5. In The Grey when the guy falls out of the tree and his daughter is standing over him with her hair draping over his face and then it cuts to the reality of wolves eating his face.
  - For me it’s the dude in the river who dies like an inch below the surface. Brutal
6. The end of Funny Games where they nonchalantly tossed that woman in the lake to drown. Even though the entire movie is about pointless cruelty, that last act struck me as particularly cruel and disrespectful.

## 3. a funny actor mostly known for comedies who is surprisingly good in serious roles?

**Score:** 7440
**Body:** Steve Carell in Little Miss Sunshine (2006)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jim Carrey, everyone knows him for his over the top comedies, but Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind and The Truman Show show he can absolutely carry a serious role with real emotional depth
  - Even going full-method in Man on the Moon.
    - "No way... That's great! WE LANDED ON THE MOON!"
2. Both John Lithgow and Michael Keaton started out in comedy and surprised me with serious roles as villains.
3. Bryan Cranston.
4. Robin Williams
5. Carell is an absolute star at portraying serious characters
  - Foxcatcher
6. Jason Bateman and Bob Odenkirk are both comedians that have put on some stellar serious acting roles.
  - Even with Saul Goodman alone he went from the funny, half comic relief character, to ,imo, one of the most compelling leading characters ever put on television.

## 4. What actor was perfectly cast for their role but was screwed over?

**Score:** 7382
**Body:** Henry Cavill as Superman and Andrew Garfield as Spider Man are prime examples of actors perfectly suited for their roles. 

If the WB actually put in more thought in creating a DC cinematic universe, and if he had a director that actually understood Superman, Cavill could’ve been the definitive Superman for the 21st century. 

As for Andrew Garfield as Spider-Man, I read that he was fired because he didn’t show up to a meeting with Sony Pictures because he was sick. That’s awful and if the Amazing Spider-Man 2 was a success, his Peter Parker could’ve made his way to the MCU

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Karl Urban as Judge Dredd and Russell Crowe/Ryan Gosling in The Nice Guys. It's a terrible shame that neither film got a sequel.
  - Two great movies that could’ve had two or three sequels. The Nice Guys got buried somehow. I’ll never understand that
  - Karl Urban as Judge Dredd was absolutely amazing. It's such a shame that movie flopped.
2. Chris Pine’s Kirk.
  - Chris Pine in Dungeons & Dragons.
    - A total gem of a movie! It's disappointing that a sequel won't happen.
3. Henry again in The Witcher
4. Timothy Dalton as James Bond. Took over the franchise when it was at a low point after the Roger Moore horse had been beaten to death. Made 2 great Bond movies but MGM shelved the franchise bc of poor box office returns.
  - Not because of box office, it was due to a legal challenge over ownership. They even wanted Dalton to return for Goldeneye, but he thought he'd aged out of the part after a 6 year gap. Such a shame as he was a brilliant Bond.
5. Matt Ryan, Constantine. Could have played him for five seasons of a gory, scary HBO version and been a huge hit. As it is, he showed up in Arrow, got his own sanitized but ok CW show, and joined as a supporting character in a different show.

## 5. Has There Ever Been an Objectively Great Actor Who Completely Fucked Their Career Than Mickey Rourke?

**Score:** 7129
**Body:** I struggle to think of another one who comes close.

Rourke came out swinging in the early 1980s’ with a series of edgy, offbeat roles in acclaimed films like Body Heat, Diner, and The Pope of Greenwich Village. Lots of actors have been called ‘the next Marlon Brando,’ but it was a more accurate for Rourke than it was for other: like Brando, he was handsome in a grittier, more unconventional way than most movie stars, and he often went for more daring roles than your traditional leading man.

He peaked in 1986-87, with 9 1/2 Weeks making him a box office draw and the double shot of Barfly and Angel Heart standing as his best performances yet.

This should’ve been the start of an incredible career, but it’s actually where the trouble starts: he develops an attitude, thinking he’s above playing the Hollywood game. He turns down meaty roles like Rain Man and Die Hard so he can do embarrassing passion projects like Johnny Handsome and Wild Orchid. He starts to become known as being difficult to work with.

In 1991, well past his marketable prime, he makes the decision to take up professional boxing, a truly baffling side project for an actor. He’s nowhere near as good a boxer as he is an actor, but he never got the hint and further alienated people from wanting to work for him. His films during this time - Harley Davidson and the Marlboro Man, White Sands, FTW - are forgettable and embarrassing compared to the films he made in his prime.

Then come the facial surgeries. I’d say mid-1995 is when the decline of his looks. He also starts taking steroids to the point where his voice loses the suave, smooth tone of his youth and drops about two whole octaves. By 1998 he is completely indiscernible as the guy from 9 1/2 Weeks. His looks severely limit the range of roles he can take and his attitude remains unchanged.

He spends most of the early 2000’s languishing in obscurity, doing made-for-tv and direct-to-video schlock. He gets a few meaty roles (Sin City, a great cameo with Nicholson in The Pledge) but nothing that changes the downward trajectory.

Then in 2008, he gets an unprecedented second chance with The Wrestler. Finally, a role that suited his current appearance and range. It’s a tour de force performance as good as any he turned out in his early career. It looked like the unthinkable had finally happened and Rourke was finally going to return to form.

Up to the Oscars, Rourke seemed to be making a genuine effort to rehabilitate his image and make good on the new opportunities afforded to him. He is charming, friendly, and even contrite in interviews. He even lays off the surgeries for a while and begins to vaguely look like his old self again. Even when he loses the Oscar to Penn, he does so diplomatically.

Then? He gets a big supporting role in Iron Man 2, and what happens? He has a not so great time making it and immediately falls back into the arrogant ‘above it all’ attitude, and resumes regularly fucking up his face with surgeries.

Nowadays he’s a complete joke and has completely fucked up his legacy. Future generations are only going to know him as a grotesque weirdo who made a few good films once. It’s a shame, because he could have been at the level of a DDL or Philip Seymour Hoffman. He could have been an undisputed great.

Anyone else you can think who royally fucked their career trajectory as spectacularly as he did? Closest I can think of is current day Johnny Depp.



### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tom Sizemore (RIP) had an outstanding run of supporting roles in the 90's - Heat, Saving Private Ryan, Natural Born Killers, True Romance, Devil in a Blue Dress, Bringing Out the Dead, Strange Days, etc. Then meth, sex tapes, beating up Heidi Fleiss and other weird shit happened.
2. Chevy Chase is a huge comedic talent. Was the biggest thing in comedy but apparently an absolute nightmare to work with.
  - Chevy Chase and Keanu Reeves are perfect mirrors of each other. One was an undisputed brilliant comic actor who was so personally unlikeable he phased out of the A-list because nobody wanted to work with him.

Keanu Reeves was generally considered a bad actor and often made fun of for roles like Bram Stoker's Dracula but was such a good guy people in the business found roles for him suited to his strengths to the point that he's arguably way more popular today than he was in the 90s. They don't make a lot of Die Hard imitators like Speed anymore. He could have easily fell into the hole that swallowed Jean-Claude Van Damme and Steven Seagal. But just being a good guy made people want to work with him and now he's John Wick.
    - Keanu's skill set is limited but he puts in the work for what he can do. Martial arts and wire work training for The Matrix films, firearms training for the John Wick films. 
      - Dont forget totally excellent guitar playing and time travel
      - I was gonna say this. It’s not just that they make roles tailored to his strengths, but also that they can depend on him because he’s very dedicated to his work and the crew. I remember the BTS for Constantine and you could tell he was super invested into the role. I don’t think he’s ever had a major role where the production had problems. He’s even saved productions before.
        - After ‘The Matrix’ became a hit, he gave a bonus to the stunt people out of his own pocket because he credited them with the movie’s success.  He’s a REALLY nice guy, by all accounts. 

And he CAN act, though he’s definitely limited.  If you haven’t seen it, check out ‘The Gift’ by Sam Raimi, with Kate Blanchett (two more reasons to see it!).  He plays a sinister redneck, and he is EVIL!!!  Like, chilling.
3. You know, RDJ, but he had a comeback. When I saw Roark was cast as Whiplash, I thought Marvel was going to become a vehicle to rehabilitate actors that had become obscure.
  - Came here to post RDJ, people completely forget he was a tremendous fuckup. He went “home” one night and went to sleep in the bed of a neighbor’s kid. He couldn’t get a job anywhere and made less than almost everyone in Iron Man and was a pain to insure.
4. Looks like that Tim Robinson character in the old man costume

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm month

## 1. Happy Gilmore 2 is a bad movie that unintentionally serves as a fascinating exploration of Adam Sandler and the nature of populism

**Score:** 1379
**Body:** Happy Gilmore 2 is (in my opinion) not a good movie, but I’m not here to write a review. The Happy Gilmore movies are zany comedies that you aren’t really meant to think about. However, with a little critical examination, the sequel is incredibly strange in that it follows the same story beats as the original while completely inverting its themes.

In the original, Happy is framed as an audience surrogate and an outsider to golf culture. Happy had no interest in golf until he, already an adult and failed hockey player, accidentally discovers his natural talent for it. He golfs wearing a hockey jersey, he swears, he gets into brawls, and his fans are typical beer-chugging sports guys. He sticks out like a sore thumb among the professional golf crowd and is only barely given a pass due to his skill.

The antagonist of the original, Shooter McGavin, represents the golf establishment. He regards Happy with a snobbish, elitist attitude, and is disgusted that Happy doesn’t fit the mold of a traditional golfer. Shooter believes that, because he ‘paid his dues’ and came up through the golf world the traditional way, he ‘deserves’ to win the championship over Happy.

So that’s the dynamic of the original. Happy is the relatable “man of the people” who sticks out amongst the preppy stuck-up golfers, and the audience wants to see him triumph over the judgmental old guard Shooter. So how is the sequel different?

Well, the villains of Happy Gilmore 2, the Maxi-Golfers, are the direct antithesis to what Shooter McGavin represented. Maxi-Golf is a start up sports league with a completely different type of golf. What Maxi-Golf actually entails is kept vague up until the climax of the movie, other than it being regarded with contempt and disgust by Happy, Shooter, and all the other established golfers.

For most of the film Maxi-Golf is represented by two characters. The first is Frank Manatee, the founder of Maxi-Golf, who is a straightforward cliched Silicon Valley CEO type (outside of a totally not tiresome running joke about him having bad breath) that needs no further explanation. The other, more interesting character is Billy Jenkins. Billy is initially introduced as an affable new pro golfer. After winning the tournament, Billy reveals that he was a Maxi-Golfer the whole time and uses his clout from winning to force the “real” golfers to a Maxi-Golf tournament that will determine the future of golf, setting up the climax of the film.

Two very important reveals happen afterwards that I must detail. First, in a move that carries incredible symbolic significance (that I don’t think the writers realized), Happy recruits Shooter to play alongside him on team Real Golf in the Maxi-Golf tournament. Second, we finally get to see what Maxi-Golf is. It’s essentially an over-the-top actionized version of golf straight out of Idiocracy. There’s pyrotechnics, loud music, crazy hazards, and gimmicky challenges like golfing off the side of a moving cart. It is very deliberately meant to seem low-brow and moronic.
What makes this so interesting to me is that, by the logic of the original Happy Gilmore, Happy and the ‘Real Golfers’ should be the villains. Happy doesn’t just team up with Shooter McGavin, he HAS BECOME Shooter McGavin. He sees something infiltrating the golf world that is at odds with golf culture, something he looks at as crude and dumb in the same way Shooter looked at him, and he feels the need to knock it down to preserve golf. Whereas the audience was meant to be frustrated by the disrespect shown to Happy in the original, they are meant to laugh along with the disrespect shown to Maxi-Golf. Happy is competing with the Real Golfers in order to pull up the very same ladder he once climbed. At no point does Happy Gilmore 2 demonstrate any awareness that it has completely inverted the first film.

So, what are we to make of this? I’m not entirely sure, and I have two possible conclusions to present:

 My first and more simple conclusion is that the contradiction between movies is unintentional commentary on Adam Sandler himself. The first Happy Gilmore was made early in Sander’s career. To paraphrase Rocky 3, Sandler was young and he was hungry. Like Happy, Sandler was someone from the outside with something to prove. Now that Sandler is thoroughly part of the Hollywood machine, not only as an actor but also the owner of a production company, he can only conceptualize the character Happy as an insider. The scene where Happy is cordially dining with other PGA champions is particularly illustrative. Sandler has gone from a guy on SNL to someone who has a seat at any film industry party or awards show he cares to attend. It’s also worth noting that Happy Gilmore 2 is stuffed with cameos by pro-golfers, something that wouldn’t be possible if the movie alienated the PGA and LIV Golf by showing them as villains. Creating Maxi-Golf may have been artistically motivated by a desire for access and a need to play nice with giants in another industry.

My second and more speculative conclusion is that the movies reflect shifting politics and the right-wing co-opting of populism. Happy is a populist figure in both movies. He is the audience surrogate, a relatable normal-ish guy in need of money. In the original he enters the golf world as a “man of the people” and butts heads with rich snobs because he refuses to follow the etiquette of their insular society, attracting a fanbase of typical rowdy sports fans along the way. In Happy Gilmore 2, Happy is once again broke and golfing to make money, but this time his populism sides with the golf establishment rather than against. Golf is under attack by a new outside force, and it’s up to Happy to defend the sanctity of golf (and we all love golf don’t we guys). Populism is a style and it can be used to support anything, even two exact opposite ideas. Populism meant being yourself and sticking it to the man. Now it means keeping the ‘freaks’ from polluting culture and making sure fellow ‘normals’ stay in charge.

I will add here that I found Maxi-Golf and its portrayal as a brand new, brightly colored, culturally destructive force made me vaguely uncomfortable. I want to be very clear that I am not accusing Happy Gilmore 2 or its creators of being bigoted. I have no objectionable content to point to. However, it gives off strange vibes, as if it’s hate propaganda that had several words changed in the script Mad Libs style to remove all the bigotry, if that makes any sense. Like if you tasked a bunch of transphobes to make a comedy movie with trans villains, they would end up making Happy Gilmore 2 but without the golfing. On that note, I haven’t watched Lady Ballers and probably never will, but if by chance anyone here has seen both movies, I’d be interested to know if there are any parallels between the two.

Thanks for reading my ramblings. I’m curious if anyone had similar thoughts, or came to different conclusions, or flat out thinks I’m wrong. I’d love to know your thoughts.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Interesting thoughts. I'd counter that Happy still represents the man of the people. Even though he is competing against Maxi Golf, he still is an outsider to traditional golf. He still plays the same way. He still dresses like a slob. When he goes back to playing again, he doesn't go to some posh country club, he goes to a run down muni tract and plays with a bunch of stoners who also act and dress nothing like traditional golfers. On top of that, he has the ultimate real life golf outsider, John Daly, living with him.

I think that the PGA dinner scene that you used to support your premise actually does a solid job of disproving it. When all the PGA players get together at the dinner, they're not acting like Shooter in the first movie, stuck up, tight, and conservative. They're acting like characters in an Adam Sandler's friends in an Adam Sandler movie, goofy and immature. Hell, Ricky Fowler makes a that's what she said joke about a fork being up Schauffele's ass. It seemed apparent to me that they were using Liv Golf as a model for their antagonist. I believe this point is driven home by the fact that Happy's team is made up of the two best PGA players and the two best Liv players.

Then, the final shot to save traditional golf is made by the ultimate outsider, Oscar, who ironically and presciently is played by Bad Bunny. If anything, Happy Gilmore isn't telling us that freaks are polluting the normal's culture, it's telling us that freaks are the new normal. All of that taken together, I think if we are really going to look this deeply into the socio-political messaging of Happy Gilmore 2, what it is telling us that freaks should be a part of normal society and that normal society should be a little more freaky. But we also have to be aware of going too far, like Maxi Golf. If Maxi is an analogy for Liv Golf, one of Liv's biggest supporters is a certain dipshit in the White House. Maxi Golf is the MAGA movement and we, the freaks and the normals, need to come together to come together and destroy it before it destroys the WORLD!

Then again, maybe they wrote the script in two hours drunk from chasing scotch with triple espressos and just needed a plot point antagonist and Maxi Golf is what they came up with.

I can't believe I just spent 15 minutes on this.
  - Interesting. So Happy's influence helped remedy the elitism in golf culture and by the time of Happy Gilmore 2 the PGA has become what it should have been all along? That is an angle I hadn't considered and makes a lot of sense.
2. I haven't seen the second movie, but now I'm interested. Excellent commentary. 

I think another example of this is the Snyder remake of Dawn of the Dead, which I read as a thematic reversal of the first. In Romero's, the mall and its shoppers are overtly criticized. Its a corrupting force and the zombies are not some strange unknowable, they are clearly somewhat regular people. There are shots where they are essentially going about their routine, doing the same things they would non-zombie. The mall, consumerism, has made them brain dead. They aren't particularly threatening, but the threat is that you would join/become brain dead too.

In Snyder's, the mall (consumerism), is not corrupting but saves the protagonists. It keeps them safe from the zombies, who unlike in Romero's, are terrifying and confusing. They are a force from the outside and the mall is a sanctuary. Its a defense of western-style capitalism rather than a criticism of it.

Now unlike Happy Gilmore, these are different directors. I am intrigued by the thematic shift you identified. I'd guess its probably unintentional but its really interesting. But like Happy Gilmore, both the films are responding to different times. Romero saw the spread of suburban culture and consumerism and criticized it. Snyder made his films at a time when the Happy 90s had died a tragic death and the United States was fighting two wars overseas,  ostensibly "defending western values." His zombies are not our friends and neighbors but "terrorists" who want to destroy everything with no reason or logic.
  - Themeatic shifts in remakes is somewhat inevitable. Even though it's a shot for shot remake, Gus Van Sant's Psycho feels like a cotton candy compared to the cold, rather austere Hitchcock classic. Van Sant even pointed out he was proving that you cannot remake something and expect it to hit the same.


Though on that, I rather like how Invasion of the Body Snatchers remakes echo the uneasy fears people have at the time. 70s with the rise in Christian nationalism and stagflation. 00s and the influence of social media on discourse. I'd imagine today one based on AI is already on the cards, but a more interesting one might be looking at how polarised political discourse is driven by fear of the other...


Anyway, back to Gilmore 2, it was a clone made with Netflix money. The popularist shift seemed almost inevitable given the current Zeitgeist. Really what would be interesting is if it had been a daft backpack trip around Scotland, and Gilmore discovering Scottish ancestry in common with McGavin, and with that the pair realise their rivalry is historically based.

Also, the exact premise of the show can kind of be considered a spoiler to the first episode, so if anyone doesn't want to read that, just go watch the pilot for yourselves.

Otherwise: >!It is essentially a new take on the idea of "body snatchers", but the body snatchers are a hive mind who act in a sort of benign benevolent way towards the Protag, who is mysteriously immune. And even though creator Vince Gilligan says AI was not on his mind when writing the show, there are a lot of parallels to be made.!<

In horror circles it's generally accepted that the remake is as-good if not better than Romero's. And, sure, technically it's a hell of a movie. But the way it uses the shape of Romero's movie to express, either intentionally or not, a reactionary worldview is something I rarely have seen discussed. Aside from what you are pointing out, just looking at who survives and how they die is very telling. How much of that comes from James Gunn's script and how much was further adapted by Snyder is an interesting question; Gunn seems like he would be on Romero's wavelength, while Snyder can't help but speak to his Randian, uber-mench reactionary instincts. And thrills and excitement aside that movie left a bad taste in my mouth.
    - Well, that's who Snyder is. Watchmen had the same issue and, lest we forget, he adapted 300, which is the quintaessential reactionary movie that enhances every negative reactionary aspect about the source comic.
      - He also was set to do an adaptation of *The Fountainhead*, which IIRC he’s described as one of his favorite books of all time.
3. This is a fantastic write up and thought exercise regarding movies that the vast majority of people don’t want to seriously engage with.  I haven’t seen Happy Gilmore 2 but I think I’ll watch it this weekend.

A quick thought before I watch the sequel:
The original is classic Slobs vs Snobs.  Happy is portrayed as a regular guy who at the end of the day is just trying to play the game that we are familiar with.
It sounds like Maxi-Golf isn’t that at all.  Sure they are “outsiders” but not every outsider is a Good Guy Underdog.  It’s interesting that you bring up bigotry/transphobia.  My thoughts are going towards Spotify, Netflix, Big Streaming etc.  They were “outsiders” to the culture industry, and people are clearly hooked on their product.  Are they underdogs?  Are they making culture better?
4. I think the key difference is Happy was an outsider as a person. But he played the sport on its existing terms, albeit in an unorthodox way. 

This isn’t the case with Maxi, which was an attempted wholesale replacement of a sport by something different, and with all the cynicism that comes with Silicon Valley. 

So it stands to reason that Shooter and Happy team up. 

It’s like how family members will fight each other but unite when someone external antagonises a family member. Theres an old joke that the only way humanity will have peace is if we get invaded by aliens. 

The principle is the same: we fight over our differences until an external factor makes us unite over our similarities. 

## 2. Frankenstein (2025) was such a disappointment

**Score:** 942
**Body:** SPOILERS BELOW!!!!

God, watching this movie legitimately made me sad. Not because it moved me but because it was such an  horrible adaptation of such a wonderful novel. I imagine if you've never read Frankenstein, you would definitely find some enjoyment out of it, but as someone who loves the book, I couldn't help but think after watching it that this movie was such a simplistic and one-dimensional retelling of a far better story.

The problem really arises from the simple black-and-white view the movie takes on Victor and his creation. I mean, the movie paints Victor as a manipulative and abusive creep. The book has none of that yet paints a picture of a far more compelling monster. Victor is a monster because he is so self-centered and self-absorbed to the point where he has little to no consideration for others.

When he first creates his monster, he doesn't start abusing him but rather runs away in horror of what he's done, giving no consideration for what may become of his creation. Later he discovers that his loved ones have been worried sick for him, as he basically hadn't told a soul what he'd been up to or where he'd been for months. When the monster tells Victor that he will see him again upon his wedding night, right after Victor destroys the female companion intended for his creation, he only thinks that he might be in danger rather than considering that the monster would go after Elizabeth. Victor in the books is a grey character, not malicious in intent or spirit but rather just a man whose carelessness and selfishness inflict so much pain on those around him.

The Monster as well is a far more shallow version than the monster presented by Shelley, and make no mistake, in the books he is a Monster. He may not have been born a monster, true, but he very much becomes one. Del Toro, however, just transforms the monster into a misunderstood victim, and in doing so, makes him lose all his agency and nuance. In a strange way this movie sort of reminds me of those Disney villain origin movies. Where you take a villainous figure and go, "Oh, here's the REAL story," but in so doing just make the character lose everything that made them interesting and engaging.

In the book the monster is a person that craves companionship, love, and affection from the others, yet he has been brought into a world that is revolted by him. People look at him only with disgust and horror, and when faced with such a reality, he lashes out at those who have hurt him, comforting himself with that at least he can inflict pain on others in return. But in the movie, he is just a poor misunderstood baby.

You can clearly see this in how the movie does in how it deals with the blind old man. In the movie the blind man gets killed by a couple of wolves; some guys find the blind man with the monster and attack him in the belief that the monster killed him. Just a poor misunderstood victim. In the book, the monster is discovered by the family living at the house, who are horrified by him and chase him away, and when the monster returns, the family has left the house. When faced with being rejected once more by people he wanted to get close to, he proceeds to burn the whole house down in anger and revenge.

The moment the movie lost me, where I couldn't bear to watch anymore, was right about the moment where Victor killed Elizabeth because it was just awful in every imaginable way. The whole notion that Elizabeth cares/has a thing for the monster just ruins the relationship between Victor and the monster and the tragedy that Victor inflicted on his creation. In the book, Elizabeth is Victor's love, whom the monster kills, along with causing the death, either directly or indirectly, of everyone Victor loves, to condemn his creator to the same fate that Victor brought upon him: to live an existence devoid of the love of others.

And with the movie establishing that Elizabeth has a thing for the monster, his tragedy no longer becomes "I live under the unbearable burden of having been created as a violation of nature, thus having no place in God's design," but rather "Oh, my daddy was mean to me and shot my crush." Like, why are you so upset, monster? You don't exist under the terrible weight of knowing that no one in the world can ever love someone like you. You just need to find another freak like Mia Goth who wants to climb you like a mountain, and everything will be alright.

Having watched this movie, I can come to no other conclusion than that Del Toro was definitely the wrong person to make this movie. Del Toro loves monsters and as such cannot see Frankenstein's monster as monstrous. He isn't a result of man's ambition gone too far; he's just "misunderstood." The movie looks great; it is well-acted and well-made. But everything about the execution of the story so utterly pales in comparison to Shelley's masterpiece that I can honestly say that this movie is the most disappointing thing I have ever seen.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For me it was the use of digital cameras and Netflix’s colour grading. The film looked whack. If it was shot on 70mm it could have been decent. Instead we got the same looking footage of Selling Sunset.
  - Why is no one talking about this? Everyone says how beautiful it looked and I completely disagree.
2. While I'm not a fan of the book particularly (having to read it many times as an A-Level settext will kill love for any book), I was also disappointed that the complexity of Victor and the Monster was stripped away. I actually found the Monster more of a tragic figure in the book because of that complexity, because of the way the world twists him into the monster they thought he was born as.


And as you said, Victor is not great person, not a likable person, but there's all sorts of layers of greyness in the book that I wish Del Toro had played with more. And the book allows for all sorts of fun readings generally. I remember some book critics enjoying how Victor tries to supplant the role of women (childbirth) and that Elizabeth's death on their wedding night before the marriage is consummated can be read as Victor's fear of women and sex.


I loved the prototype monsters and the over-the-top sets and costumes. But it was a dolls house - because the characters were so flat. I love Pan's Labyrinth very much (it's a top ten film for me) but the Good vs Evil worldview in that film fits the fairytale themes of the story. (That, and The Faun himself is an ambivalent figure - we are at turns frightened of him and comforted by him. So Del Toro can create ambiguous monsters!). In Del Toro's more recent works, the lack of complexity in his characters, the huge neon arrows pointing at the Baddies vs the Goodies really puts me off (I loathed The Shape of Water).


What also really ticked me off was the Byron quote at the end. Of course Byron is involved in the history of the novel. But that the most influential science fiction novel of all time was written by a young woman (and the daughter of a feminist writer!) does feel important to me and for all of Byron's talents, he very much feels like the antithesis of all that.


I feel bad for being so critical because GDT is such a lovely man and so passionate about films. But his last few films have not been for me.
  - the byron quote felt SO weird to me
  - Ugh, the Byron quote. I turned to my friend (we watched it at her house) and was like, do NOT quote Byron at the end of this. It felt weirdly insulting to Shelley.
3. My issue with Del Toro's last two monster flicks is that I find their anthropocentrism really boring. The monster in Frankenstein hints in one scene that he has the memories and maybe spirits of the dead men he's composed of inside him. Instead of getting an exploration of how an intelligence like that might differ from our own we get "oh, it's actually not that different, all you need is love." In Shape of Water, same deal. The creature is only as alien as a blind date who hasn't arrived to dinner yet.
  - I found the notion of the monster dreaming in the minds of the people he's constructed from to be a super compelling idea. Too bad it was never expanded upon or brought up again at any later point in the film.
4. I think the source material is very personal to Del Toro and what he carries with him is the relationships between the monster and its creator and the world around it. I think that's the movie we have. I don't agree with expecting him to faithfully adapt the book, but rather I would say we should explore what he's saying in his adaptation.

Alternatively, I believe he said it's not at AI parable, but it kind of works as one! Megalomaniac tech bro creates AI to feed his ego, gets trained on Reddit shit posts, and goes sideways.
  - I don't really expect an adaptation to be 'faithful' to the original as a unique take is what make adaptations interesting. However I do think Del Toro stripped the original of its nuance and did not replace it with anything particularly substantial. *That* makes it a poor adaptation, in my opinion.
5. I heard someone say "If you want to see Guillermo Del Toro's FRANKENSTEIN, you're going to be disappointed. But if you're going to GUILLERMO DEL TORO'S Frankenstein, you'll probably be delighted." I haven't seen it yet but from I've heard, that's a good thing to keep in mind before watching it.

## 3. One Battle After Another is So Good Because it Looks Real at a Time Where Movies Look Fake

**Score:** 918
**Body:** Among the innumerable reasons why OBAA succeeds as much as it does is the way 'it' *looks.* 'Cinematography' is the go-to buzz word used to praise a film's visuals and while OBAA is visually stunning, that isn't what I'm referring (solely) to. I seriously cannot stop thinking about how impactful the character design and costuming has been in dictating my outlook on the film.

(Contradiction) Briefly focusing on the cinematography, PTA avoids the [recurring trend](https://youtu.be/tvwPKBXEOKE) of intense close-ups, shallow depths of field and blurry backgrounds in favor of wide shots, lighting 'mistakes', and non gimmicky one-takes to induce immersion. I cannot overstate just how refreshing it was to watch a movie without the [repulsive close-up](https://i.redd.it/hexiqp3vc54c1.jpg) that plagues contemporary cinema.

PTA's use of close-ups still allows you to scan the surroundings and retain your immersion because of the awareness that there is a 'real' world [behind](https://imgur.com/a/nYB7OeX) the character being focused in on. Take these sets of [close-ups](https://imgur.com/a/t0HYIpk), for example, as representing the 'proper' way to center a character without entering the realm of [uncanny, artificial valley](https://imgur.com/a/2JA85f6). OBAA makes you feel as if you can actually step into a lived-in world, *our*, lived-in world because that's where the characters are, and the immersion is so strong, that you never question this for even a second.

We need more movies that aren't afraid to make their characters look real, look ugly, and look grimy. OBAA, as gorgeous of a movie as it is, features some of the most unflattering close-ups because it understands that authenticity, feeling, take precedence over perfect makeup and flattering close-ups. The characters actually fucking sweat, get hurt, have dry skin when they should, wear real and unflattering lazy outfits etc.

To some, this might be a minor aspect of what makes the movie so great but I truly feel that ***this is*** what contributed to what is the rawest, most culturally fitting film of the last 15 years.

## 4. Theaters are struggling largely due to the quality of Hollywood movies

**Score:** 430
**Body:** There are many factors that are contributing to the demise of movie theaters, but why do so many people choose to ignore one of the most obvious reasons?

The common reasons that are parroted include the society-wide adoption of streaming services, the in-theater behavior of audiences, the rising ticket and concession prices, the availability of home theater equipment etc..

Sure, these all contribute in some capacity to the decline of movie theaters, but what is conveniently ignored is how they are symptoms rather than the cause.

Hollywood doesn't make movies like they used to, and many people realize this. Why spend all this money gambling at your local theater when Hollywood's track record over the last 20 to 30 years has been so rotten. Nowadays, people have nearly the entire catalogue of films from every studio at their fingertips, which only constantly reinforces their skepticism about the latest theatrical releases. I don't place most of the blame on the theaters, it is Hollywood that is gutting them, especially when you consider how bottom of the barrel the competition is from streaming services.

PG Rated movies are selling out theaters this year. The younger demographic is clearly interested in seeing movies in theaters, despite not growning up in a time when theatergoing was a societal norm. So much for the excuses that young people would rather stay at home with a tablet or phone watching youtube, playing video games, or scrolling social media. Zootopia 2, Minecraft, Lilo & Stitch are dominating the box office.

If anything, it is older audiences who have increasingly checked out, but who can blame them? They know how much better Hollywood movies were decades ago, and they can just rewatch them at home.

## 5. The Running Man: why would a unique director wilfully become generic?

**Score:** 361
**Body:** I watched The Running Man earlier in the week, and thought it was a perfectly functional, decent action-comedy. I also can’t remember the last time I’ve been more baffled by the existence of a film, to the point where it felt like a director was performing self-sabotage for us all to see. When I watch a bad film from the likes of Zack Snyder, I can still understand the intent behind the piece, and the fact that as a director, he is playing to his own sensibilities.

But The Running Man is the kind of disappointment that truly baffles me. Edgar Wright is a highly distinctive director, and unlike Wes Anderson, there has been no expression of fatigue from the audience, whatsoever. Despite this, Wright decided to forsake his own style. I appreciated Last Night in SoHo (even though it jumped the shark in the final act), because there, it still felt like Wright traded off his style to try something distinctive. But with The Running Man, it truly baffles me to no end why a director like Wright would, as a conscious decision, make a film that feels like a David Leitch B-side.

There are two moments in this film which show that, to no surprise, Wright is still capable of bringing his unique sensibilities to the film. One which involves bullets fired to the beat of a song, and another in which Glen Powell’s face moves in the same direction as his face on a TV behind him. Both moments were shown in the trailer, and are not indicative at all of the rest of the film. If Wright is still capable of creating such moments (I mean, why wouldn’t he be?) then why choose not to? Again, I would understand and support if he abandoned his traditional style to aspire to something (like what PTA did with One Battle After Another, or Spielberg with Schindler’s List), but this is not one of those instances. It is a talented director striving toward mediocrity.

EDIT: My [full review](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WZulA36AdGU) of the movie!

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think a big problem is that he made an unnecessary rod for his own back by sticking to the formula of the book too much.

The book is a depressing, dystopian tale of a desperate and sickly man who survives more often than not by being shuttled around, and hidden by others.

Wright wanted to make a zany action comedy about a tough, charismatic guy with anger issues, who fights his way out of trouble.

The problem is that he tried to map the latter concept onto the former, despite the fact it can't support it, and made little to no changes to accomodate it. It'd be like shooting The Shining as a comedy. But changing nothing fundamental about the story or structure. None of your beats are going to work.

Wright was simply a bad fit for the material. Even the 80s version (which has plenty of one-liners and satire) understands that the material doesn't work unless everyone involved treats the stakes as deathly serious.

But Wright (as much as I love him) seemingly can't make a film unless it's drenched in irony. Everyone is a cartoon character. And this works when the script and story are in perfect harmony with his vision. But when it's fighting tooth and nail against it, you get this. Neither fish nor fowl.

It's the equivalent of steak-flavoured ice cream. Two things that are great in isolation. But absolutely do not work together. Meaning the project was fundamentally misconceived from the start. It just couldn't pick a lane.
  - These were my thoughts too on watching it. It's a fascinating example of the challenges in adapting a novel to screen. People have long criticised the 1987 version for "being nothing like the book" but I've always felt it was brilliant in how it distilled the core ideas into something that worked much more cinematically for that time. Wright was so restrained in trying to please/impress King while also trying hard to please his audience. The two sensibilities cancel each other out for the most part, leading to a strangely moot, grey experience.  


But my biggest confusion is the ending. How could it follow the book so closely, but then botch the ending so absolutely terribly? It has no resonance or coherence, it's just flat-out awful. I'm aware he was still editing it up to the later half of October, he must have had some truly tortuous nights if that ending is the best they could put together. 
    - That was my partners take. That ending just killed everything that came before it. It never hit high points but it was a great representation of the core story. Until the end when it decided to become something it wasn’t.
2. He’s been wanting to adapt The Running Man for a while. So clearly his heart was in it at some point.

Unfortunately the movie just didn’t come together. You ask why a director would make the conscious decision to make a mediocre movie (assuming that’s what you meant by “David Leitch b-side” — unless you really liked Bullet Train) … I mean, they usually don’t? 

I think what may have happened is that he made the decision to tone down his usual style from the get-go, but because this was a big studio blockbuster, he was also pressured to make some changes. I have a strong suspicion the (horrible!) ending was the result of some last minute rewrites and reshoots but who knows. Maybe that was always the intent, and he was just misguided.
3. 'unlike Wes Anderson, there has been no expression of fatigue from the audience, whatsoever'

Anderson has actually become a much more consistent performer, even if on a mid-tier level, than Wright. Wright is less productive than Anderson, and seems to have even lost his cult following.
4. Filmmaking is really fucking hard.


And the attention to detail in his films is really a lot of work.


At some point, you just don't have the energy or the inclination to keep doing the same thing over and over just because that's what people expect.


That's without going into the business side of things, which can limit time and freedom.
  - When you’re dealing with a decent budget you have to answer to the money supply
    - There are directors with final cut but it looks like Edgar Wright may not be among them anymore.
5. Sometimes you do a film for the check or to establish a relationship for a film you want to do later. Sometimes the producers have final say and you just do it and move on. He will be 100% okay.
6. I haven’t seen it yet, but I read the book recently to prepare. From previews it looks like it’s trying to be fun. The book is bleak as hell. 

To me, this is like turning Cormac McCarthy’s The Road into a buddy comedy.

Plus Glen Powell sucks.
